questions.question: 1 (a) All variables, constants and other identifiers should have meaningful names. (i) You recorded information for the estimate of the cost of building a computer in Task 1. Give a data structure that you created for Task 1, its name, data type and use. Data structure ....................................................................................................
questions.text: 1 (a) All variables, constants and other identifiers should have meaningful names. (i) You recorded information for the estimate of the cost of building a computer in Task 1. Give a data structure that you created for Task 1, its name, data type and use. Data structure ....................................................................................................

parts.answer: One mark for method, one mark for an extension or reason. Many correct answers, an example is given. Use a previously stored number//generates/uses an initial value (1) Update it (by 1) every time an estimate is made (1) 0478/21 May/June 2018 five from: 1 Initialise (stock level) flag 2 Check stock level for the chosen processor type 3 Only check RAM if processor available // Only check processor if RAM available 4 Check stock level for the chosen type of RAM 5 Finish process if problem with (RAM/Processor) stock levels 6 Identify out of stock (processor/RAM)//Set flag to appropriate value 7 Identify stock level OK//Set flag to appropriate value 0478/21 May/June 2018 foundProc ← FALSE count ← WHILE NOT foundProc AND count <=3 DO IF processor(estNo) = proc(count) AND stProc(count) > 0 THEN foundProc ← TRUE ENDIF count ← count + 1 ENDWHILE IF foundProc THEN foundRAM ← FALSE IF RAM(estNO) = RAM1 AND stRAM1 >0 THEN foundRAM ← TRUE stRAM1 ← stRAM1 - 1 ENDIF IF RAM(estNO) = RAM2 AND stRAM2 >0 THEN foundRAM ← TRUE stRAM2 ← stRAM2 - 1 ENDIF ENDIF IF NOT foundProc THEN OUTPUT "Processor out of stock" ELSE stProc(count) ← stProc(count) - 1 ENDIF IF NOT foundRAM THEN OUTPUT "RAM out of stock" ENDIF 0478/21 May/June 2018

One mark for each correct point (max 5): Explanation 1 How the number of orders was calculated 2 Deal with the case where the estimate has not been turned into an order 3 Calculating the total number of each component sold 4 Details of method actually used to calculate numbers of components 5 How the total value of all the orders was calculated 6 Display summary 7 Display complete summary of number of orders, total number of components and total value of orders Programming statements can be used but must be explained to gain credit. 0478/21 May/June 2018 Section B six from: 1 Initialisation of counters for positive numbers and zeros 2 Appropriate loop for 1000 iterations 3 Input number inside loop 4 Test for positive numbers 5 Update positive number counter 6 Test for zeros 7 Update zero counter 8 Output counters with appropriate messages outside loop zero ← posCount ← FOR count ← 1 TO 1000 INPUT number IF number > 0 THEN posCount ← posCount + 1 ENDIF IF number = 0 THEN zero ← zero + 1 ENDIF NEXT OUTPUT posCount, " positive numbers " OUTPUT zero, " zeros "
Reduce the number of iterations to a manageable amount // Simulate the input (e.g. random generation) 0478/21 May/June 2018

Digit(1) Digit(2) Digit(3) Digit(4) Digit(5) Digit(6) Digit(7) Digit(8) Sum OUTPUT 5 7 0 1 2 3 4 6 44 GTIN-8 Digit(1) Digit(2) Digit(3) Digit(4) Digit(5) Digit(6) Digit(7) Digit(8) Sum OUTPUT 4 3 1 0 2 3 1 0 30 GTIN-8 One mark for data entry – both sets of digits 1–7 One mark for both Digit(8) One mark for each Sum (max Two ) One mark for both OUTPUT three from 1 Change first loop to 8 iterations 2 Check that the input Digit(8) is equal to the calculated Digit(8)... « if equal output check digit correct « otherwise output check digit incorrect Or 1 Change first loop to 8 iterations 2 Put all 8 digits through the algorithm to calculate Sum ... « if MOD(Sum,10) is equal to zero, check digit correct « otherwise output check digit incorrect 0478/21 May/June 2018 One mark for each (max three ) 10.00 boundary/erroneous data // the price should be rejected // value is out of range 9.99 boundary/extreme/normal data // the prices should be accepted // value is within normal range ten erroneous/abnormal data // input should be rejected // value is wrong type There are many possible answers. e.g.: Totalling is used to sum a list of numbers (1) Counting is used to find how many numbers/items there are in a list. (1) Totalling example (1) e.g. Total = Total + Number Counting example (1) e.g. Counter = Counter + 1 Records two from: Length check Type check Presence check Format check 0478/21 May/June 2018
Field: Type Sold Out Date Title Table: PERFORMANCE PERFORMANCE PERFORMANCE PERFORMANCE Sort: Show:  ; ; Criteria: Like “Jazz” False or: One mark per correct column.
